idea.hook: Our Milky Way: A Galactic Cannibal?!
facts: Galaxies aren't static; they frequently merge and 'absorb' smaller galaxies.
Our own Milky Way is actively absorbing the Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy right now, stripping its stars and gas.
Astronomers have identified remnants of at least 5 other dwarf galaxies previously consumed by the Milky Way.
idea.payoff: The Milky Way grows by devouring its neighbors – a cosmic feast spanning billions of years!
idea.title: Milky Way: A Cosmic Cannibal?! 🤯🌌

scenes.text: Our Milky Way: A Galactic Cannibal?!
scenes.visual: A vibrant, rotating Milky Way galaxy, with a smaller, faint galaxy subtly approaching from the side.
A larger spiral galaxy visually pulling streams of stars and gas from a distorted dwarf galaxy.

scenes.text: The Milky Way is absorbing the Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy right now!
scenes.visual: An illustration of the Milky Way, with a clear stream of stars and gas visibly being stripped from the Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy.

scenes.text: Remnants of at least 5 other dwarf galaxies previously consumed are still visible.
scenes.visual: A detailed map or artistic rendering of the Milky Way's halo, highlighting faint, wispy trails and star clusters that are remnants of past absorptions.

scenes.text: The Milky Way grows by devouring its neighbors – a cosmic feast spanning billions of years!
scenes.visual: A grand, slightly enhanced view of the Milky Way, slowly rotating and subtly growing in luminosity, representing its ongoing 'feast' and growth.
